coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent nanocrystalline cellulose/carbon dot hydrogels
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wei
Wang, Sichun
Li, Ying
Ma, Chunhui
Huang, Zhanhua
Wang, Chunsheng
Li, Jian
Chen, Zhijun
Liu, Shouxin
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent nanocrystalline cellulose/carbon dots (NCC/CDs) hydrogels were successfully prepared by a facile one-step green hydrothermal carbonization process. The properties of NCC/CDs hydrogels prepared at different temperatures were investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and thermogravimetric analysis. NCC/CDs hydrogels with high crystallinity, high thermal stability, and excellent fluorescence properties can be obtained by controlling the hydrothermal treatment temperature. Small rod-like NCC/CDs hydrogels with a diameter of 2–6nm, a length of 40–60nm, and the cellulose Ι structure were obtained when the hydrothermal carbonization temperature was 240°C. The NCC/CDs hydrogels exhibited an excellent broad spectral response and high fluorescence stability at various pH values. Considering of the wide natural abundance of NCC and the biocompatibility of the NCC/CDs hydrogels, Preparation in this simple, green approach is attractive for future bio-medical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocrystalline cellulose
Hydrogels
Fluorescence
Hydrothermal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and size distribution of NCC and hydrothermally carbonized NCC samples
Structural analysis of NCC and hydrothermally treated NCC samples
Thermal stability of NCC and hydrothermally carbonized NCC samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572017CB12, 2572017EB03), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31500467, 31570567, 31670592), the Special Fund for Forest Scientific Research in the Public Welfare (201504605), the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province for Young Scholars (QC2015034), and Jiangsu Key Lab of Biomass-based Green Fuels and Chemicals (JSBGFC14012).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Storage modulus and (b) loss modulus as a function of angular frequency at 25°C for suspensions with NCC and hydrothermally carbonized NCC; angular frequency dependence of storage modulus, loss modulus and loss tangent of NCC and hydrothermally carbonized NCC: (C) NCC; (d) NCC180; (e) NCC200; (f) NCC220; (g) NCC240 and (h) NCC260.
(a), (b) PL emission and excitation spectra of NCC220 and NCC240, and emission spectra of (c) NCC220 and (d) NCC240 at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) PL spectra of NCC220 excited at 360nm in solutions at different pH; (b) Linear calibration plot and correlation coefficient with different pH, Inset is a photograph of the NCC220 under ambient light and illuminated by a UV lamp with 365nm light; (c) PL spectra of NCC240 excited at 375nm in solutions at different pH, and (d) Linear calibration plot and correlation coefficient with different pH, Inset is a photograph of the NCC240 under ambient light and illuminated by a UV lamp with 365nm light.
Bright-field and fluorescence microscopy images of (a) NCC220 and (b) NCC240 on excitation at 365, 440, and 550nm.
Morphology and corresponding diameter and length distributions of (a) NCC, (b) NCC220, and (e) NCC240; (c), (d)TEM images of CDs in NCC220; (f), (g) TEM images of CDs in NCC240.
Schematic model of the hydrothermal synthesis of NCC/CD fluorescent hydrogels.
(a) FT-IR, (b,d) XRD patterns and crystallinity spectra, (c) 13C CP/MAS NMR spectra of NCC and hydrothermally carbonized NCC samples.
(a) TG and (b) DTG curves of NCC and hydrothermally carbonized NCC samples.

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent nanocrystalline cellulose/carbon dot hydrogels

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science and Engineering College, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Max-Planck-Institut für Polymerforschung, Ackermannweg 10, 55128, Mainz, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nanocrystalline cellulose/carbon dots (NCC/CDs) hydrogels were successfully prepared by a facile one-step green hydrothermal carbonization process. The properties of NCC/CDs hydrogels prepared at different temperatures were investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and thermogravimetric analysis. NCC/CDs hydrogels with high crystallinity, high thermal stability, and excellent fluorescence properties can be obtained by controlling the hydrothermal treatment temperature. Small rod-like NCC/CDs hydrogels with a diameter of 2–6nm, a length of 40–60nm, and the cellulose Ι structure were obtained when the hydrothermal carbonization temperature was 240°C. The NCC/CDs hydrogels exhibited an excellent broad spectral response and high fluorescence stability at various pH values. Considering of the wide natural abundance of NCC and the biocompatibility of the NCC/CDs hydrogels, Preparation in this simple, green approach is attractive for future bio-medical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanocrystalline cellulose (NCC), prepared by acid hydrolysis of cellulose, showed lots of applications in different areas including regenerative medicine (Fleming, Gray, & Matthews, 2001), optics (Iwamoto, Nakagaito, Yano, & Nogi, 2005), and composite materials (Ruiz, Cavaille, Dufresne, Graillat, & Gerard, 2001). Recently, hydrogels based on NCC or its derivatives were proposed and well developed in bio-medical applications, such as drug delivery, tissue engineering applications, etc, due to the good mechanic property and biological minimal toxicity. In these hydrogels, NCC served as gelation precursor or reinforcement reagent. For example, Surface-modified NCC were used as a reinforcing agent to prepare injectable carboxymethyl cellulose/dextran composite hydrogels (Yang, & Pan, 2010).

As bio-medical materials, NCC composite hydrogels with fluorescence is an ideal property so that they can be visualized and located accurately in the cell or tissues. An approach to constructing NCC composite hydrogels with fluorescence is to modify NCC with organic fluorescent unit. Many methods were developed to decorate NCC with organic fluorescent chromophores. For example, Dong and Roman (2007) used a three-step method to NCC label with fluorescein-5′-isothiocyanate (FTIC). Mahmoud et al. (2010) used a similar method to label NCC with a range of fluorophores, including FTIC. A two-step method was used to attach FTIC and a pyrene-based fluorophore to aminosilane-treated NCC Filpponen, Sadeghifar, and Argyropoulos (2011) used TEMPO oxidation, carbodiimide amidation, and copper(I)-catalyzed azide–alkyne click chemistry to add coumarin and anthracene fluorophores to NCC surfaces. Finally, Nielsen, Eyley, Thielemans, and Aylott (2011) modified NCC with responsive fluorophores using one- and three-step procedures. However, chemical reagents were involved in all of the above-mentioned approaches. As a result, it was difficult to separate the fluorescent nanoparticles from the left chemicals, which will introduce toxic reagent into the materials and reduce its biological compatibility. Moreover, even if the left chemicals can be separated completely, the organic chromophores introduced does not have good optical stability, which is a general problem for organic chromophores. Thus, it is difficult to develop a bio-friendly method to prepare fluorescent NCC composite hydrogels via grafting organic chromophore to NCC chemically.

Carbon dots, as a new type of material for biological imaging, have good optical properties, especially the good optical stability. Carbon dots have been demonstrated for successful uses in the fluorescence imaging of cells, which take advantage of their visible excitation and emission wavelengths, their nontoxic from cytotoxicity, their fluorescence brightness of individual dot and high photostability (Bourlinos et al., 2008; Li, Kang et al., 2012; Luo et al., 2013; Zhang, Sun, & Wu, 2015). Carbon dots can be fabricated from lots of natural products, such as carboxymethylcellulose (Wu, Li, Tan, Wu, & Liu, 2015), starch (He et al., 2011), watermelon peel (Zhou, Sheng, Han, Zou, & Li, 2012), banana juice (De & Karak, 2013), orange juice (Sahu, Behera, Maiti, & Mohapatra, 2012), willow leaves (Gao et al., 2014), bamboo leaves (Liu, Zhao, & Zhang, 2014) and potatoes (Xu, Zhou, Liu, Dong, & Huang, 2014), etc. via hydrothermal carbonization. Hydrothermal carbonization is an environmentally friendly method that does not use organic solvents and can be carried out under mild conditions in a sealed container. The shape and chemical structure of the products can also be controlled by using hydrothermal carbonization. Additionally, hydrothermal carbonization also showed its priorities in fabrication of NCC composite hydrogels (Lewis, Derakhshandeh, Hatzikiriakos, Hamad, & MacLachlan, 2016). These results encouraged us to use hydrothermal carbonization to prepare a hydrogel containing NCC and NCC-derived carbon dots (NCC/CDs) which served as gelation reagents and fluorescent units respectively in a simple way.

In our work, NCC solution was treated with hydrothermal carbonization at different temperature. After treatment of hydrothermal carbonization, NCC was partially converted to NCC-derived carbon dots (NCC/CDs). The left NCC served as gelation reagent of hydrogel and NCC-derived carbon dots played roles as fluorescent units. Thus, fluorescent hydrogel containing NCC/CDs (NCC/CDs hydrogel) can be prepared in one-step method via hydrothermal carbonization. The NCC/CDs hydrogel prepared in our work had stable fluorescent emission. The straightforward hydrothermal method developed here does not require any addition of reagents or solvents to control the crystal structure and fluorescent properties of the resulting NCC/CDs hydrogel.

Commercial bleached kraft softwood pulp (85% International Standardization Organization brightness) used as the raw material to prepare NCC was provided by a paper-making factory in Heilongjiang, China. Analytical-grade sulfuric acid was purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

Kraft softwood pulp was first milled and then passed through a 0.5-mm screen to ensure uniform particle size. An aqueous NCC suspension was prepared mainly by following the sulfuric acid hydrolysis method reported previously (Dong, Revol, & Gray, 1998). The milled pulp was hydrolyzed in sulfuric acid (64wt%; 8.75mL of sulfuric acid solution per gram of pulp) at 45°C for 30min with vigorous stirring. The cellulose suspension was then diluted, centrifuged, washed, and dialyzed against distilled water until the exterior water reached neutrality before further use.

In a typical experiment, the NCC suspension (1.5wt%, 30mL) was added to a stainless-steel autoclave with a volume of 50mL. The autoclave was heated in a furnace at 180, 200, 220, 240, or 260°C for 4h, and then allowed to cool to room temperature. The resulting brown hydrogel was collected and centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 2×10min to remove black solids. The obtained brown hydrogel was then subjected to dialysis (1000 molecular-weight cut off) for about 48h to obtain the NCC/CD hydrogel. The NCC/CD hydrogels prepared at temperatures of 180, 200, 220, 240, and 260°C are denoted as NCC180, NCC200, NCC220, NCC240, and NCC260, respectively.

The rheological behavior of NCC and hydrothermally carbonized NCC samples were conducted on a stress-controlled AR-2000 rheometer (TA Instruments, USA) with two different geometries: plate/plate geometry (diameter40mm, gap 1mm). The values of strain amplitude were monitored to ensure the dynamic oscillatory deformation within the linear viscoelastic region, which is confirmed by strain sweeps. The strain amplitude value was set as 0.1 for all the samples. For frequency sweeps, measurements were performed from 0.1 to 100rad/s at 25°C. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed on an LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a 120-W xenon lamp as the excitation source. Fluorescence microscopy images were obtained using an inverted fluorescent microscope (Zeiss Axiovert 200, Germany). The excitation wavelengths were 365, 440, and 550nm, and the light source for the fluorescence microscopy experiments was a mercury lamp with a fluorescent filter. All fluorescence measurements were performed at room temperature. pH tests were conducted using the same NCC/CD solution concentrations over a pH range of 2.25–12.84. The solution pH was adjusted using buffer solutions of different pH. TEM observation of NCC, NCC220, and NCC240 samples was performed using a FEI Tecnai G2 microscope at an accelerating voltage of 80kV. An NCC sample droplet (5μL) consisting of the diluted slurry was dropped on a carbon-coated electron microscopy grid, and then negatively stained with 1wt% phosphotungstic acid solution to enhance the image contrast. The NCC220 and NCC240 samples were not negatively stained; they were simply directly dropped on the carbon-coated grid for observation. TEM images of the carbon dots were obtained on a JEOL 2011 (FEI, Holland) that was operated at 200kV. FT-IR spectra were measured using a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR instrument (Thermo Scientific, USA). The spectra were measured in attenuated total reflectance mode, and data were recorded over the range from 650 to 4000cm−1 with a resolution of 1cm−1. XRD measurements were acquired on a D/max-r B X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.154nm) at 40kV and 30mA. The samples were scanned over a 2θ range from 5° to 40°. The crystallinity index C I of the cellulose materials was calculated using the Segal method (C I (%)=(1−I am/I 200)×100), where I 200 is the peak intensity of the (002) lattice diffraction at 2θ =22.6°, and represents both the crystalline and amorphous regions of the material, and I am is the diffraction intensity of the amorphous fraction at 2θ
= 18°. The degree of polymerization (DPη) of NCC/CD hydrogels was measured using the viscosity method with an aqueous 1.0M bis(ethylenediamine)-copper (II) hydroxide solvent, as described by Iwamoto (Iwamoto, Nakagaito, & Yano, 2007). The 13C CP/MAS NMR analysis of the samples was carried out at room temperature with a Bruker DRX-400 spectrometer. Spectra were acquired with a 4-mm MAS probe using a combination of CP, MAS, and high-power proton decoupling methods. A total of 800 scans was accumulated for each sample. TG analysis was performed using a TGA-Q50 TG analyzer (TA, USA). Temperature programs for dynamic tests were run over the range of 25–600°C at a heating rate of 10°C/min.

NCC and hydrothermally carbonized NCC were subjected to frequency ramping study. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a. For NCC and NCC260, G’ shows frequency independent characteristic at a low angular frequency band and transforms to be frequency sensitive at a high angular frequency band. The difference suggests that a high frequency increases the hydrogen bonding of the NCC and NCC260 with a lower concentration and the rate of formation of the inherent network, thereby leading to an increase in G’. For NCC180, NCC200, NCC220 and NCC240, the G’ remains relatively independent of frequency throughout the entire angular frequency ramp. This result may be due to a decrease in the surface charge of the individual NCC which leading to gelation. The gelation network structure formed was stable enough to resist the angular frequency even at 100rad/s. A similar tendency for loss modulus (G”) versus frequency can be observed in Fig. 1b. In NCC (Fig. 1c), there are two cross-over frequencies (G’ and G” cross-over), which normally denotes a reorganization or change of structure/phase. G’>G” and the loss tangent (tanδ) remained <1 at a low angular frequency because of the less cross-links and weak network structure, which represents a visco-fluid behavior. When the NCC were treated by hydrothermally carbonized, as shown in Fig. 8d–g, G’, G”, all increased with frequency, and G’>G”, showed a visco-elastic feature. For NCC180, NCC200, NCC220, NCC240, G’ are always larger than G” and there is none cross-over between them, and tanδ <0.3 throughout the entire frequency ramping study, which might mean that the NCC had been already in a gel form. For NCC260, it shows a visco-fluid behavior same as NCC due to the excessive hydrolysis during the higher hydrothermal temperature (260°C). All the above mentioned results indicate that the hydrothermally carbonized temperature is the mainly influential factor to produce NCC/CD hydrogels and maintenance of gel behavior of the NCC/CD hydrogels.

As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, the emission of NCC220 (excitation at 360nm) and NCC240 (excitation at 375nm) appeared at around 400–500nm with a peak at 458nm, and at around 400–560nm with a peak at 489nm. For NCC220, there is a clear peak centered at 360nm in the excitation spectrum with an emission wavelength of 458nm. For NCC240, the signal is centered at 375nm and has an emission wavelength of 489nm. The PL spectra also show that the maximum emission wavelength and intensity are directly related to the excitation wavelength, which is characteristic for CDs (Wu, Li, Wu et al., 2015; Baker & Baker, 2010).

Fig. 2c and d showed the emission spectra on increasing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 600nm. For NCC220, on changing the excitation wavelength from 330 to 590nm, the emission peak red-shifts from 450 to 730nm with a gradual decrease in intensity. Changing the excitation wavelength for NCC240 from 320 to 600nm also results in a red shift of the emission peak (from 450 to 775nm) with a gradual decrease in intensity. Visible emission ranging from blue to red region was observed when the same NCC/CDs sample was excited by UV and visible light. These PL properties can be attributed to surface energy traps formed through incomplete reactions, and the radiative recombination of electrons and holes (AnilKumar et al., 2013). Additionally, polycyclic aromatic compounds within the samples may be responsible for the excellent PL properties (Liang, Ma, Shi, Li, & Yang, 2013).

The relationship between the emission properties and the pH of the NCC/CDs samples was studied using buffer solutions (pH 2.25–8.08, pH 9.21–10.89, pH 12.84). The solution color changed from light to deep yellow as the pH was varied from 2.25 to 12.84, as shown in the insets of Fig. 3 b and d. This result may be a result from changes in the surface properties and electronic transitions of the NCC/CDs. pH increase only decreased the emission intensity of the samples but did not influence the wavelength (Fig. 3a and c) which was consistent with previous reports (Liang et al., 2013). Acidic pH neutralized the electronics on NCC/CD surface with Oxygen-containing moieties. While, basic pH increased the negative charge density on the surface of NCC/CD. This increased negative charge density on the surface further influenced the π–th* and n–e* electronic transitions, chemical properties, and surface defects on the NCC/CDs, which finally led to fluorescence quenching. Linear calibration plots with high correlation coefficients of R2 =0.9686 and 0.9939 were obtained for NCC220 and NCC240, respectively. Although the fluorescence intensity decreased as the pH increased, the decrease in the fluorescence intensity was small, and thus indicated the excellent fluorescence stability of these NCC/CD hydrogels.

Fig. 4 displayed optical fluorescence microscope images of NCC220 and NCC240. NCC220 and NCC240 were prepared by placing a drop of sample solution on a glass slide and covering it with a glass cover slip (Fig. 3). The NCC/CDs hydrogel exhibited emission colors of blue, green, and red when was excited at 365, 440, and 550nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and size distribution of NCC and hydrothermally carbonized NCC samples
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of NCC and NCC/CDs hydrogels were characterized by TEM. The TEM image of NCC shows massive fibers with a diameter of 5–10nm and a length of 140–260nm, as illustrated in Fig. 5 . Interestingly, we found that NCC220 and NCC240 did not require negative staining for TEM characterization, which may be due to the fact that the faint yellow NCC hydrothermal liquid product is an excellent negative staining reagent. NCC220 has a diameter of 4–8nm and a length of 70–105nm, whereas NCC240 has a smaller diameter of 2–6nm and a shorter length of 40–60nm. For NCC220 and NCC240, CDs combined with the NCC can be clearly observed (inset of Fig. 5b and e). The decrease in both the diameter and length of the NCCs may be caused by the hydrothermal reaction occurring at the surface and inner amorphous regions of the cellulose. Since the fluorescent property stems from the carbon dots, the CDs TEM images of NCC220 and NCC240 are presented in Fig. 5c, d and f, g, respectively. This shows that the CDs are similar spherical structure. The crystalline nature of the CDs was examined by HRTEM and lattice planes with a spacing of 0.34nm were observed in the crystalline structures. This is similar to that of the (002) diffraction plane of sp2 graphitic carbon (Baker & Baker, 2010).

And also, the DPη of the NCC was 148, after hydrothermal treatment for 220°C and 240°C, the DPη of NCC220 and NCC 240 were 85 and 44, respectively. This revealed that about 43% (NCC220) and 70% (NCC240) long-chain polysaccharides of NCC were hydrolysis during hydrothermal treatment processing. The surface and inner amorphous regions of the cellulose were partly hydrolyzed to polysaccharides and monosaccharides at the hydrothermal reaction at high temperatures. These polysaccharides were then further hydrolyzed into monomers. They can also yield fructose through isomerization, and then undergo dehydration and fragmentation reactions that lead to the formation of various soluble products, such as hydroxymethylfurfural derivatives, 1,2,4-benzenetriol and aldehydes (Aida et al., 2007; Chheda, Huber, & Dumesic, 2007). Subsequent polymerization or condensation reactions induced by intermolecular dehydration or aldol condensation between these intermediate products (furan and benzene derivatives) then occur to give the final CDs (Pu, Hu, Huang, Davison, & Ragauskas, 2013; Titirici, Antonietti, & Baccile, 2008; Yao et al., 2007). These are condensed polyaromatic and furfural structures that were connected through condensation, polymerization, linear linking, or decarbonylation via an ether bond, and contain alkanes, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups at the ends of their side chains. At this stage, the fluorescent NCC/CD hydrogels were formed (Fig. 6 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis of NCC and hydrothermally treated NCC samples
ce:section.ce:para: The peaks at 3200, 1560, 1418, and 1035cm−1 in FT-IR spectra (Fig. 7 a) of NCC, NCC180, NCC200, NCC220, and NCC240 indicated that NCC and hydrothermal NCC (180–240°C) retain the main functional groups of cellulose (Li et al., 2016). No new absorption peaks appeared in the FT-IR spectra of the samples after hydrothermal carbonization, indicating that the hydrothermal treatment (180–240°C) did not change the main structure of cellulose. However, the OH stretching vibration and deformation vibration peaks at 3200 and 1035cm−1 became weaker with the increase of hydrothermal temperature. This is because hydroxyl groups are easily degraded to intermediate products during hydrothermal carbonization. When the hydrothermal temperature was 260°C, the peaks at 3200 and 1035cm−1 disappeared, which indicates that NCC underwent complete hydrothermal carbonization. The peak at 1594cm−1 revealed that condensed CC groups were present in this sample, whereas a signal consistent with aromatic CH out-of-plane bending vibrations at 735cm−1 indicated the presence of aromatic rings. The adsorption band at 1683cm−1 corresponds to the carboxylate groups. These results thus suggest that dehydration and aromatization occurred during the hydrothermal treatment of NCC.

The changes in the crystal structure and crystallinity of the samples during hydrothermal carbonization were analyzed using XRD. The XRD patterns of the samples exhibited diffraction peaks at around 2θ =16° and 22.6° (Fig. 7b,d), which confirmed that the type I crystal lattice of NCC was preserved after hydrothermal treatment at 180–240°C. The C I of the samples was calculated according to the Segal method (Li, Yue, & Liu, 2012; Li, Zhao, & Liu, 2013). The C I values of NCC180, NCC200, NCC220, and NCC240 were 84.36%, 88.25%, 88.74%, and 83.21%, respectively, showing increases of 7%–12% over that of NCC (76.15%). This increase in crystallinity undoubtedly results from the removal of the amorphous regions during hydrothermal treatment. A broad reflection peak centered at 23.5° is present in the XRD pattern of NCC260, indicating that it has an amorphous carbon structure.

To further illuminate the structural transformation of NCC during hydrothermal carbonization, the NCC and hydrothermal samples were characterized by 13C CP/MAS NMR spectroscopy (Fig. 7c). The spectra of all of the samples displayed the typical signals from cellulose, which were assigned as follows: the C1 (104.6ppm), C2/C3/C5 (71.9 and 75.3ppm), C4 (89.2ppm), and C6 (65.4ppm) peaks correspond to the carbon atoms of the glucopyranose rings in the crystalline regions, whereas the C4 (84.5ppm) and C6 (63.1ppm) peaks correspond to those in the amorphous areas (Tang et al., 2013). The spectra of the hydrothermally treated samples showed no obvious changes compared with that of NCC. This result indicated that the hydrothermally carbonized samples retain the cellulose structure up to a hydrothermal temperature of 240°C. The intensities of the C6 peaks of NCC180, NCC200, NCC220, and NCC240 (63.1ppm) were weaker than that of NCC, which indicates that the amorphous phase was removed during the hydrothermal process. For NCC260, peaks were observed in the region between 0 and 100ppm, which is characteristic of sp3 carbon atoms, and indicates the presence of aliphatic and ether carbons. Signals were also observed between 100 and 170ppm; this region is characteristic of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in CC double bonds, and indicates that NCC underwent hydrothermal aromatization and carbonization. More specifically, the signals between 100 and 120ppm can be attributed to the oxygen-bound OCC groups within the furan moieties (Baccile, Laurent, Babonneau, & Fayon, 2009; Falco et al., 2011). The region between 180 and 220ppm is characteristic of CO groups in carboxylic acid, ketone, or aldehyde moieties. These NMR results were in accordance with the FT-IR and XRD analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of NCC and hydrothermally carbonized NCC samples
ce:section.ce:para: TG and differential thermogravimetric (DTG, represents the corresponding rate of weight loss for NCC and hydrothermally carbonized NCC(180–260°C) were studied (Fig. 8 ). For all samples, a small weight loss corresponding to the evaporation of adsorbed water was observed at low temperatures (<110°C). The degradation of NCC was observed as two main pyrolysis processes in the DTG curve. The first process between 195 and 301°C dominated the overall pyrolysis, and the second occurred between 301 and 450°C. The thermal decomposition of NCC occurred at low temperature and over a wide temperature range, indicating that NCC has low thermal stability. In contrast to those of NCC, the TG and DTG curves of the hydrothermally carbonized NCC samples showed one main pyrolysis process. The starting decomposition temperatures of NCC180, NCC200, NCC220, NCC240, and NCC260 were 293, 306, 311, 332, and 329°C, respectively. These results indicate the increased thermal stability of the hydrothermally carbonized NCC samples compared with that of NCC. This may be caused by the increased crystallinity and decreased content of surface hydroxyl groups in the hydrothermally carbonized NCC samples. The thermal stability of NCC260 was lower than that of NCC240; this may be caused by the absence of the cellulose crystal structure in this sample.

We prepared NCC/CDs hydrogels with fluorescence via one-step green hydrothermal carbonization method. The temperature effects were investigated for the formation of NCC/CDs hydrogels. At a hydrothermal carbonization temperature of 240°C, smaller rod-like NCC/CD nanomaterials with diameters of 2–6nm and lengths of 40–60nm, and that retained the high crystallinity of the cellulose Ι structure were obtained. The prepared NCC/CDs hydrogels showed high crystallinity, high thermal stability, and excellent fluorescence properties. NCC/CDs hydrogels prepared in this new method has a great protential in future bio-medical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572017CB12, 2572017EB03), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31500467, 31570567, 31670592), the Special Fund for Forest Scientific Research in the Public Welfare (201504605), the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province for Young Scholars (QC2015034), and Jiangsu Key Lab of Biomass-based Green Fuels and Chemicals (JSBGFC14012).
